notes: Layer 1 question-type taxonomy for LÄS (Swedish reading). Eight types covering literal retrieval, inference, author stance, rhetorical purpose, characterization/summary, contrast (skillnadsfrågor), logical entailment / consequence, and conditional 'och i så fall'-format. Synthesized from direct corpus reading of 540 LÄS prompts across 28 exams (the candidates extractor only surfaced one cluster, so the entries below are grounded in hand-tagged qids rather than statistical clusters).

attack_protocol: Note the trigger phrase 'enligt texten' / 'enligt textförfattaren' / 'av texten framgår' — the answer must be stated in the passage, not inferred.
Identify the named entity, study, person, year, or claim the question pins down (e.g. 'VA-nämnden', '1977 års tillträdesreform', 'Sten-Olof Ullströms avhandling').
Scan the passage for that entity and read the surrounding sentence carefully — do not paraphrase from memory.
Match the option that restates the passage's wording most faithfully. Reject any option that adds an idea the text does not contain, even if it sounds plausible.
If two options seem to match, prefer the one whose scope (degree, time, agent) is identical to the text — wrong options usually overgeneralize or shift the agent.

common_distractors.pattern: True-sounding world knowledge not stated in the text
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Students fill the gap with what they know from biology/law/history class. The HP only credits what the passage says.
Mentions a name from the text but pairs it with a fact that belongs to a different name (e.g. swaps the recensent's claim with the avhandling's claim).
Text says 'en bidragande orsak' / 'kan vara'; distractor says 'huvudorsaken' / 'är'. Sounds like a fair summary, isn't.

common_distractors.pattern: Adjacent fact from the same paragraph
common_distractors.why_it_traps: The wrong fact sits next to the right one in the passage; students see the keyword and grab the nearest plausible sentence.

attack_protocol: Note the inference trigger: 'av texten att döma', 'tycks', 'mest sannolikt', 'mest rimlig', 'antyder', 'utifrån resonemanget'.
The answer is NOT verbatim in the text — but it must follow from what the text says without adding outside premises.
Locate the passage segment the question points to and articulate the underlying premise in your own words.
Test each option: does it follow from the premise using only information in the passage? Eliminate options that need an extra assumption the text never establishes.
Prefer the most cautious option that still answers the question — strongly worded inferences are usually traps.

common_distractors.why_it_traps: Reads as 'common sense' but the premise needed isn't in the passage.

common_distractors.pattern: Too-strong conclusion from a hedged premise
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Passage says 'kan tyda på' / 'en bidragande faktor'; the option converts it to a categorical claim.
True given the passage in some abstract sense, but doesn't actually answer what the question asks.
Swaps cause and effect from the passage's chain ('X ledde till Y' rewritten as 'Y orsakade X').

attack_protocol: Identify whose stance the question asks about: textförfattaren, recensenten, or a named third party introduced in the text (Spivak, Berglund, Posner, Heller Sahlgren …).
Mark the evaluative vocabulary the text uses about that person/object — 'kritisk', 'beundrande', 'skeptisk', 'efterlyser', 'invänder', 'försvarar', 'beklagar'.
Build a one-word valence for the stance (positive / negative / mixed / agnostic). Most LÄS stance answers are mixed or qualified, not pure.

Eliminate options whose valence disagrees with the textual cues. Then between same-valence options, pick the one whose scope matches what the author actually evaluates (the method? the conclusion? the writing style?).
If the question is 'I vilken fråga är X kritisk?' or 'Vad är X kritisk till?' — locate the specific object of criticism, not the general topic of the text.

common_distractors.why_it_traps: Author is critical, but of something else (the framing, not the conclusion; the data, not the theory).
Confuses recensentens stance with the avhandling-författarens, or with a quoted opponent.
Text says 'med viss tvekan'; option says 'avvisar'. The hedged author becomes a categorical one.

common_distractors.pattern: Neutral-sounding option when the author is clearly partisan (or vice versa)
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Smooths out a sharp stance into bland balance to feel 'safer'.

attack_protocol: Spot the purpose trigger: 'vad vill textförfattaren ha sagt med …', 'vad vill X understryka / illustrera', 'vilken funktion fyller …', 'i syfte att', 'vad framstår som syftet'.
Ignore the surface content of the cited example/sentence/quote. The question asks what RHETORICAL JOB it does (illustrate a claim? concede a point? introduce a contrast? signal irony?).
Find the surrounding claim the device supports. The example/quote is almost always evidence FOR a thesis stated nearby.
Pick the option that names the function relative to that thesis, not the option that paraphrases the example's content.
If the device is a metaphor or an analogy, the answer is usually the abstract claim it concretizes — never the literal subject of the metaphor.

common_distractors.why_it_traps: Paraphrases the metaphor / quote at face value instead of naming what it argues for.

common_distractors.pattern: Plausible authorial intent, but not the local function
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Names a goal the author has somewhere in the text, but not the job of THIS sentence/quote.
Says 'underbygga' when the device is actually a concession; says 'ifrågasätta' when it's confirmation.
Treats the device as ornament ('för att göra texten levande') when it actually carries argumentative weight.

attack_protocol: Spot the trigger: 'hur kan man bäst beskriva / karakterisera / definiera / sammanfatta X', 'vilken alternativ rubrik …', 'vilket svarsförslag sammanfattar bäst texten', 'texten som helhet'.

Decide whether the target is a whole-text characterization (rubrik, huvudpoäng, helhet) or a sub-object (en roman, ett begrepp, en persons utrikespolitik). Whole-text questions demand a thesis that covers the recurring theme across paragraphs; sub-object questions demand the bundled-up portrait of one entity.
List the 3–5 traits the text repeatedly attaches to the target. The right option will hit most of them; wrong options will pick one episode or one trait.
Eliminate options that describe a single example or paragraph instead of the recurring theme. (Trajectory rule: 'look for the recurring theme, not the episodes'.)
Eliminate options that capture the topic but not the angle. A correct rubrik names BOTH what the text is about AND what the text claims about it.

common_distractors.pattern: True of one example, false of the whole
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Picks up the most vivid anecdote (an episode) and uses it as the description — but the text's recurring theme is broader.
Names what the text is about ('skolbibliotekarier') but not what the text argues ('att de behövs mer än någonsin').
Adds a property the text never establishes — e.g. calls a balanced review 'avfärdande'.
Reads as good academic prose ('en mångfacetterad analys') but is so generic it can't be falsified — usually wrong.

attack_protocol: Spot the trigger: 'vad skiljer X från Y', 'i vilken fråga skiljer sig X och Y', 'skillnaden mellan', 'vad utmärker X jämfört med Y'.
Build a two-column mental table: claim about X | claim about Y. Pull from the passage; ignore your priors about the entities.
The right answer is the dimension on which the text DRAWS THE CONTRAST — even if other differences exist in the world, only the textual one counts.
Reject options where the asymmetric claim is true of one side but the text doesn't say it's FALSE of the other side. A real skillnad needs both halves explicit or strongly implied.
Watch for cases where X and Y agree on the asked dimension; the right answer is sometimes 'no clear difference / the texts converge here', or a more carefully scoped contrast.

common_distractors.pattern: True about X, silent about Y
common_distractors.why_it_traps: The option states a real property of X but the text never says Y differs on it — so it's a property, not a contrast.

common_distractors.pattern: Difference that exists in the world but not in the text
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Students rely on background knowledge of Piaget vs Vygotskij rather than what THIS text says about them.
Names a real textual contrast but swaps which side has which property.
Names a sub-detail that flows from a deeper contrast — but the question asks for the principled split, not the symptom.

attack_protocol: Spot the trigger: 'vilken blir följden / konsekvensen', 'vad skulle innebära', 'rimlig slutsats', 'om man följer textens resonemang', 'logiska konsekvens', 'rimligaste tolkningen'.
Identify the premise the question hands you (explicitly stated in the text — sometimes quoted in the question stem).
Apply the author's own reasoning, not your own — even if you'd disagree with their logic, the answer is what THEIR argument entails.
Trace one step forward from the premise. Most LÄS entailment questions are one inferential step, not a chain.
Eliminate options that don't follow even if the premise is true, and options that follow only under an outside assumption.

common_distractors.why_it_traps: Sounds logical, but uses your background reasoning, not the author's stated chain.

common_distractors.pattern: Two-step entailment when only one step is licensed
common_distractors.why_it_traps: The option follows IF you grant one extra inferential leap the text doesn't make.

common_distractors.pattern: Restates the premise as if it were the consequence
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Sounds like it answers because the keywords match — but it's the input, not the output.
Logically valid but at odds with the value-judgment the author has already committed to — students forget the author's stance constrains acceptable conclusions.

attack_protocol: Spot the trigger shape: 'Ger texten någon förklaring/svar till X, och i så fall vilken/vilket?' or 'Tar X ställning, och i så fall vilken?'. The question is two questions chained: (a) does the text address this at all, (b) if yes, what is the answer.
First settle (a) using the passage. If the text is silent or only hints, the correct option is the 'nej' option — even if a 'ja, …' option sounds plausible.
If (a) is yes, you still must match (b) to what the text actually says — the trap is picking a 'ja' option with the wrong content.
Beware the asymmetric option set: usually one 'nej' option plus 3–4 'ja, X' options. The mere presence of many 'ja' options is not evidence that the answer is 'ja'.
Read the text for the exact scope the question pins down (e.g. 'någon förklaring TILL DETTA' — not just any explanation in the text).

common_distractors.pattern: 'Ja' option that gives a true fact from the wrong place in the text
common_distractors.why_it_traps: The fact appears in the passage but doesn't answer the specific 'varför/hur' the question pins down.

common_distractors.pattern: 'Ja' option that infers what the text only hints at
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Tempting because students feel they must commit to a position — but if the text is genuinely silent, 'nej' is the answer.

common_distractors.pattern: 'Nej' option when the text does give an explanation
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Students who skim miss the explanation and default to 'nej'. The check is to find the explanation explicitly before ruling 'nej' out or in.

common_distractors.pattern: 'Ja' option that gives the wrong half of a two-sided treatment
common_distractors.why_it_traps: Text presents both sides; option commits to only one. Often the right answer is 'nej, inget entydigt svar' or a 'ja' that captures the duality.
